query: current guidelines and efficacy of pazopanib in treating VHL-associated renal cell carcinoma

retrieved_docs.title: Retinal Vascular Anomalies (VHL, Cavernous Hemangioma, Wyburn-Mason) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The results of several case reports have suggested that combined anti-vascular endothelial growth factor (anti-VEGF) agents and photodynamic therapy may hold promise as a therapeutic option for certain retinal hemangiomas. [91] Belzutifan is a HIF-2-α inhibitor and is the first Food and Drug Administration (FDA)-approved systemic oral drug for VHL-associated tumors such as renal cell carcinoma, CNS hemangioblastoma, and pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors. The most common adverse effects include anemia, nausea, headache, and raised blood glucose, and it is harmful in pregnancy. [92] Sunitinib, a tyrosine kinase inhibitor, has shown promising results in treating renal carcinomas in VHL syndrome but has not been effective in treating hemangioblastomas. [93] However, disease progression, fatigue, and hand-foot syndrome were noted in patients with advanced VHL disease. [94] Dovitinib, a tyrosine kinase inhibitor of the VEGF receptor and fibroblast growth factor, also resulted in adverse effects such as maculopapular rash, diarrhea, and fatigue. [95] Pazopanib has demonstrated clinical responses in renal tumors of VHL despite adverse effects such as diarrhea, hypertension, fatigue, and transaminitis. [96]

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Clear Cell Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Recent Update
retrieved_docs.content: In an update published in February this year, the European Society of Medical Oncology has endorsed the use of combination therapy with pembrolizumab and axitinib as the frontline therapy for treatment of naïve advanced disease, based upon the results of the Keynote 426 trials, where this combinatorial therapy was compared with sunitinib, irrespective of the IMDC subgroup and the PD-L1 biomarker status. While the same update also makes a recommendation in favor of the use of ipilimumab and nivolumab in the intermediate and low-risk settings after analysis of the long-term survival results (32 months), VEGF targeted tyrosine kinase inhibitor treatment is recommended after progression on PA/IN combinatorial regimens. Targeted therapy is also indicated when the above-mentioned combinatorial therapy cannot be used or is contraindicated. [121]

retrieved_docs.title: Clear Cell Renal Carcinoma (Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Stages 1 to 3 are characterized as localized, and stage 4 is advanced disease. The treatment for localized disease is surgery with curative intent. Radical nephrectomy or partial nephrectomy is preferred depending upon the extent of the disease, patient's comorbidities, and underlying renal function. Cryotherapy and radiofrequency ablation are reserved for unresectable or nonsurgical candidates presenting with a small renal mass. Tyrosine kinase inhibitors (sunitinib and pazopanib) are used in metastatic ccRCC as they act by inhibiting VEGFR. The mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR inhibitors) everolimus and temsirolimus have been approved for metastatic ccRCC. [6] mTOR inhibitor, along with lenvatinib (VEGFR inhibitor), is used as a second-line agent in metastatic ccRCC. The use of selective HIF2 blockers is under investigation.

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Clear Cell Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- First-Line Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Good risk disease—standard care: sunitinib, pazopanib, interferon, bevacizumab, and tivozanib. Other options include high-dose interleukin 2, bevacizumab, and low-dose interferon. Intermediate risk disease – standard care – nivolumab and ipilimumab. Optional treatments include sunitinib, pazopanib, cabozantinib, tivozanib, a combination of bevacizumab, and interferon. Poor risk disease – standard care – nivolumab and ipilimumab; optional treatments include cabozantinib, sunitinib, pazopanib, and temsirolimus. [60]

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Clear Cell Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Cabozantinib
retrieved_docs.content: It is a multitargeted receptor tyrosine kinase inhibitor that inhibits VEGF, AXL, and MET kinase receptors. AXL and MET receptors have been postulated to be responsible for resistance to VEGF-targeted therapy, granted FDA and EMA approval for patients with progression on first-line VEGF targeted therapy. The inactivation of the Von Hippel Lindau gene leads to the activation of various angiogenic pathways, which may be blocked specifically by cabozantinib. The substrate of cytochrome P 450 enzyme system 3A4, predominantly metabolized in the liver. It is administered in a daily oral dose of 140 mg, with a predicted half-life of 55 hours. ESMO magnitude of clinical benefit scale ranking of 3. [98] [104] [105]

retrieved_docs.title: Lenvatinib -- Indications -- FDA Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Radioactive iodine-refractory DTC Unresectable or advanced HCC Advanced RCC Endometrial cancer (in combination with pembrolizumab)

retrieved_docs.title: Clear Cell Renal Carcinoma (Archived) -- Medical Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: Following surgical resection, there is no clear role for chemotherapy or immunotherapy in renal cell carcinoma. Tyrosine kinase inhibitors (sunitinib and pazopanib), mTOR inhibitors (everolimus and temsirolimus) have been approved for metastatic ccRCC. mTOR inhibitor, along with lenvatinib (VEGFR inhibitor), is used as a second-line agent in metastatic ccRCC.

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Clear Cell Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Neoadjuvant Systemic Targeted Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Targeting venous tumor thrombi with an attempt to reducing their size and making them amenable for resection is an approach that is yet to find any favor. It has been a purely experimental approach and cannot be recommended outside of a clinical trial setting. [90] [91]

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Clear Cell Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Adjuvant Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: A pooled analysis of various trials using Sunitinib (S-TRAC, ASSURE), Sorafenib (ASSURE), and pazopanib (PROTECT) in the adjuvant setting has failed to show any benefit in disease-free survival or overall survival in those with intermediate/high-risk local disease or those who have undergone complete resection for localized renal cell carcinoma. It is found that high-dose adjuvant treatment in high-risk diseases may be associated with favorable disease-free survival, at the risk of incurring higher-grade adverse effects. [89]

retrieved_docs.title: Bevacizumab -- Indications -- FDA-approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic renal cell carcinoma: Bevacizumab has received approval to treat metastatic renal cell carcinoma in combination with interferon alfa.

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Cancer -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Stage 4: Systemic targeted molecular therapies have replaced immunotherapy (e.g., interferons-α) using tyrosine kinase inhibitors for a vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF-TKIs) (e.g., sunitinib, sorafenib, pazopanib, and axitinib) or rapamycin inhibitors for mammalian targets (e.g., temsirolimus and everolimus). Nephrectomy followed by immunotherapy improved survival in patients with metastatic RCC compared to immunotherapy or TKIs alone. Follow-up consists of pretreatment CT or MRI of chest, abdomen, and pelvis, followed by repeat imaging every 6 to 16 weeks, modified by clinical status. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Lenvatinib -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Lenvatinib is a multiple receptor tyrosine kinase inhibitor demonstrating potent antiangiogenic properties indicated as monotherapy or combination therapy for certain malignancies. Lenvatinib is FDA-approved for radioactive iodine-refractory differentiated thyroid cancer (DTC), unresectable or advanced hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC), and advanced renal cell carcinoma (RCC).

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Angiomyolipoma -- Treatment / Management -- Indications for Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Medications that inhibit the mTOR pathway can interrupt tumor progression and promote the reversion of the existing lesions. [5] [12] Some recommendations suggested that all hereditary angiomyolipoma lesions 3 cm or larger be treated with mTOR inhibitor medications, even if asymptomatic. [1] [5] [83]

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Clear Cell Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Axitinib
retrieved_docs.content: It is a multitargeted receptor tyrosine kinase inhibitor, which inhibits VEGFR 1, 2, 3, PDGFR alpha and beta, and c-kit—administered orally in a dose of 5 mg twice daily, with a bioavailability of 58 percent. Metabolized, primarily in the liver, has a half-life of 2.5 to 6 hours and is excreted in the urine (less than 1 percent). Approved for use in the second-line setting by the FDA and EMA in 2012. ESMO Magnitude of clinical benefit scale ranking of 4. [98] [101] [102] [101]

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Clear Cell Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Second-Line Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment options depend upon the initial treatment used in the frontline setting, either a tyrosine kinase inhibitor or immunotherapy with a combination of nivolumab and ipilimumab. If a tyrosine kinase inhibitor was used in the first-line setting, the standard options include nivolumab, cabozantinib. Other optional treatments include axitinib, everolimus, or a combination of lapatinib and everolimus. If the combination immunotherapy regimen of nivolumab and ipilimumab was used in the frontline setting, the following treatment recommendations need to be considered – Any Tyrosine kinase inhibitor or a combination of lenvatinib and everolimus. [60]

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Clear Cell Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Pazopanib
retrieved_docs.content: A multi-target receptor tyrosine kinase inhibitor which inhibits VEGFR, PDGFR, FGFR, and c-kit. Administered orally in a dose of 800 mg per day, with an oral bioavailability of 21.4 percent (13.5 to 38.9 percent). 99.9 percent protein-bound, mostly to albumin and, to a lesser extent, to alpha1 acid glycoprotein. Mainly excreted unchanged through the feces as the parent compound (in an unchanged form). A minor fraction (2.6 percent) is excreted via urine. ESMO magnitude of clinical benefit scale ranking of 3. [98] [103]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 835
retrieved_docs.content: Nephrectomy; partial or complete, referred to in Arabic as استئصال الكلية, is a surgical procedure that involves the removal of part or all of a kidney. This operation is frequently performed in the setting of renal neoplasms, specifically الورم الأورمي الكلوي (renal oncocytoma) and سرطان الخلايا الكلوية (renal cell carcinoma). The frequent co‑occurrence of استئصال الكلية with both الورم الأورمي الكلوي and سرطان الخلايا الكلوية highlights the clinical practice of managing these kidney tumors through partial or total kidney resection, informing treatment planning and patient counseling.

retrieved_docs.content: Partial or complete nephrectomy, described in Arabic as استئصال الكلية, is a specific surgical procedure that involves the removal of all or part of a kidney. This operation is a subtype of the broader category of nephrectomy, encompassing both total and partial kidney resections. Recognizing that partial or complete nephrectomy falls under the general concept of nephrectomy is essential for clinical decision‑making, surgical planning, and patient counseling regarding indications, risks, and postoperative management.

retrieved_docs.content: Benign arteriolar nephrosclerosis and essential hypertension are clinically similar conditions, as both involve pathological changes in small arterial vessels that lead to elevated blood pressure and related end‑organ effects. Recognizing the overlap in their clinical presentations helps clinicians differentiate primary hypertension from secondary renal vascular disease, guides appropriate diagnostic evaluation, and informs management strategies aimed at controlling blood pressure and preserving kidney function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2863
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant arteriolar nephrosclerosis is clinically similar to hypertension with complications and secondary hypertension, indicating that the severe renal vascular changes seen in malignant arteriolar nephrosclerosis share comparable clinical manifestations, risk factors, and disease mechanisms with complicated primary hypertension and hypertension that arises secondary to other underlying conditions. This similarity underscores the importance of recognizing overlapping signs and symptoms when diagnosing and managing patients with advanced hypertensive organ damage and secondary causes of elevated blood pressure.
